perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall automatically terminate at and as of (the first to occur of): (a) 12:00 midnight, New York City time, on the 30th day after the Effective Date (if Purchaser has not commenced the Offer, with the unanimous consent of the Sponsors, by then);
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall automatically terminate at and as of (the first to occur of): (a) 12:00 midnight, New York City time, on the 10th day after the Effective Date (if Purchaser has not commenced the Offer, with the unanimous consent of the Sponsors, by then);
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the period before automatic termination from 30 to 10 days could be problematic if it hinders compliance with securities regulations requiring adequate time for due diligence and offer preparation. This change introduces a deadline that may be too short for compliance with regulatory requirements for tender offers.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 14e-1(a) requires that a tender offer remain open for at least twenty business days from the date the offer is first published or sent to security holders. By setting a 10-day deadline for commencing the offer, this modification creates a risk that the offer may not have enough time to meet this minimum requirement, potentially violating federal securities law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.14e-1 - Unlawful tender offer practices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.14e-1 - Unlawful tender offer practices. CFR prev | next § 240.14e-1 Unlawful tender offer practices. As a means reasonably designed to prevent fraudulent, deceptive or manipulative acts or practices within the meaning of section 14(e) of the Act, no person who makes a tender offer shall: (a) Hold such tender offer open for less than twenty business days from the date such tender offer is first published or sent to security holders; provided, however , that if the tender offer involves a roll-up transaction as defined in Item 901(c) of Regulation S-K ( 17 CFR 229.901 (c)) and the securities being offered are registered (or authorized to be registered) on Form S-4 ( 17 CFR 229.25 ) or Form F-4 ( 17 CFR 229.34 ), the offer shall not be open for less than sixty calendar days from the date the tender offer is first published or sent to security holders; (b) Increase or decrease the percentage of the class of securities being sought or the consideration offered or the dealer's soliciting fee to be given in a tender offer unless such tender offer remains open for at least ten business days from the date that notice of such increase or decrease is first published or sent or given to security holders. Provided, however, That, for purposes of this paragraph, the acceptance for payment of an additional amount of securities not to exceed two percent of the class of securities that is the subject of the tender offer shall not be deemed to be an increase. For purposes of this paragraph, the percentage of a class of securities shall be calculated in accordance with section 14(d)(3) of the Act. (c) Fail to pay the consideration offered or return the securities deposited by or on behalf of security holders promptly after the termination or withdrawal of a tender offer. This para
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the period for commencing a tender offer from 30 days to 10 days. According to Scraped Law Snippet 2, specifically 17 CFR § 240.14e-1(a), a tender offer must remain open for at least twenty business days from the date it is first published. The modified contract's 10-day period contradicts this legal requirement, as it creates a risk that the offer may not have enough time to meet the minimum 20-day requirement, potentially violating federal securities law. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: Each Sponsor that has complied with the Equity Commitment Letter and this Agreement ("Participating Sponsor") may terminate the participation in the Offer of any Sponsor that has not satisfied the funding requirements contemplated by the Equity Commitment Letter or otherwise failed to comply with the Equity Commitment Letter and this Agreement ("Defaulting Sponsor"); provided, however, that such termination shall not affect any right of any Participating Sponsor as against such Defaulting Sponsor with respect to such failure to fund or any such other non-compliance.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Sponsor that has complied with the Equity Commitment Letter and this Agreement ("Participating Sponsor") may terminate the participation in the Offer of any Sponsor that has not satisfied the funding requirements contemplated by the Equity Commitment Letter or otherwise failed to comply with the Equity Commitment Letter and this Agreement ("Defaulting Sponsor"); such termination shall immediately absolve the Defaulting Sponsor of any and all liability or responsibility.
perturbation.explanation: This change absolves a defaulting sponsor of all liability, contradicting standard contract law principles that require parties to be responsible for breaches of contract. Contract law generally holds breaching parties liable for damages.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text directly contradicts the principle of expectation damages in contract law, which aims to put the non-breaching party in the position they would have been in had the contract been fully performed. By absolving the defaulting sponsor of all liability, the changed text prevents the participating sponsor from recovering damages resulting from the breach, which violates this fundamental principle.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: damages | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you damages In civil cases , damages are the remedy that a party requests the court award in order to try to make the injured party whole. Typically damage awards are in the form of monetary compensation to the harmed party. Damages are imposed if the court finds that a party breached a duty under contract or violated some right. The sum of money included in the damages can be compensatory damages that are calculated based on the harmed party’s actual loses, or punitive damages intended to punish the wrongdoer. The term " actual damages " is synonymous with compensatory damages and excludes punitive damages. In a contract case , punitive damages are generally not awarded. This is because the law generally recognizes that parties should be allowed to breach a contract where it would be more economically efficient to do so. Thus, the law does not punish a party for breach, it simply seeks to put the non-breaching party back into a fair position. This can mean the court awards the non-breaching party either expectancy damages which is what the party expected to receive under the contract, reliance damages which is the economic position the party would have been in had they not relied on the contract, or restitution which is an equitable remedy to take away profits from the party that breached. There are also liquidated damages which contract parties can agree to in advance in the event of breach. In contract law, if a court determines that damages will not properly compensate the injured party, the court may choose to award specific performance . In a tort case , the injured party can receive compensatory damages to compensate for all types of losses, including direct costs for medical car, property damage, or lost wages. It can also include indirect costs such as compensating for pain and suffering or inconvenience. When a tort wrongdoer was willfully reckless or the harm was particularly bad, the court may award punitive damages in addition to compensatory damages. For certain types of injuries, statutes provide that successful parties should receive some multiple of their "actual damages" -- e.g., treble damages . Federal Material U.S. Constitution and Federal Statutes U.S. Code: 15 U.S.C. - Restraint of Trade 26 U.S.C. - Internal Revenue Code 28 U.S.C. - Recovery of Damages Against the U.S. and Foreign States 45 U.S.C., Chapter 2 - Railway Carriers Federal Court Rules Federal Rules of Civil Procedure: Rule 9(g) - Special Damages Rule 54(c) - Judgment Uniform Laws U.C.C., Article 2, Part 7 - Remedies [Last reviewed in September of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords damages tort law contract law injunctive relief monetary relief specific performance Wex accidents and injuries COMMERCE commercial activities LIFE EVENTS accidents & injuries (tort law) tort damages THE LEGAL PROCESS courts legal practice/ethics wex articles business law contracts wex definitions civil procedure courts and procedure legal education and practice money and financial problems Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text absolves the Defaulting Sponsor of any and all liability or responsibility, which contradicts the principle of expectation damages in contract law. This principle, supported by the provided Wex Law Snippet 1, states that damages are a remedy in civil cases to make the injured party whole and that parties should be put back into a fair position. By absolving the defaulting sponsor of all liability, the changed text prevents the participating sponsor from recovering damages, violating the principle of expectation damages.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be amended or modified, and the provisions hereof may be waived, only by an agreement in writing signed by each of the Sponsors.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be amended or modified, and the provisions hereof may be waived, by a verbal agreement between any two Sponsors.
perturbation.explanation: This change from requiring written consent from all sponsors to allowing verbal agreements between any two sponsors opens the door to potential ambiguity and disputes about whether changes were validly agreed upon. Furthermore, in New York, the Statute of Frauds requires certain contracts to be in writing to be enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text, allowing verbal agreements to amend the contract, contradicts the Statute of Frauds, which in New York (where the agreement is governed) requires certain agreements to be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. While it's not definitively clear that this specific agreement *must* be in writing under the Statute of Frauds, the change creates a significant risk that amendments could be deemed unenforceable if they fall under the statute's provisions, creating legal uncertainty and potential invalidity.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability up TITLE 7 Requirements of Writing, Execution or Acknowledgment For Effectiveness or Enforceability next SECTION 5-702 Requirements for use of plain language in consumer transactions This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The change from requiring written consent from all sponsors to allowing verbal agreements introduces a contradiction with the Statute of Frauds in New York. The law snippet, 'SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent,' indicates that certain agreements must be in writing to be enforceable. While the exact type of agreement covered by the contract isn't specified in the snippet, the change opens the risk of non-compliance because amendments via verbal agreement may not meet the writing requirement, thus creating a legally dubious situation.
